title: Peppermint foot cream
subtitle: A thick, emulsified foot cream with shea butter, coconut oil, and peppermint essential oil
excerpt: Peppermint essential oil gives this foot cream a cooling, tingling sensation that works well on tired feet. Emulsifying wax binds the water and oil phases into a thick cream that absorbs without leaving feet slippery. Making emulsified creams is intermediate work: both phases must be heated to the same temperature before combining.
sourceNotes: Standard o/w emulsion base: water 60 to 70%, oils 20 to 25%, emulsifying wax NF 5 to 7%. Peppermint (Mentha piperita) essential oil at 2% for foot cream (higher than for face products; feet are less sensitive). Preservative at 1% (Optiphen or equivalent). Both phases heated to 70 to 75°C before combining to ensure emulsification.

recipe.yieldDescription: ~100 g foot cream (fills a 100 ml pot or pump)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale all phases proportionally. Keep emulsifying wax at 6% and preservative at 1%.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow the cream to cool and set fully before jarring — at least 1 hour.
recipe.temperatureNote: Heat both phases to 70 to 75°C before combining. Temperature parity is essential for a stable emulsion.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months at room temperature with preservative. Keep the lid closed. Discard if the cream separates, smells off, or the texture becomes grainy.

glossaryTerms.definition: A blend of fatty alcohols and surfactants that allows water and oil to form a stable, homogeneous cream. Without it, water and oil separate on standing.
The oil-soluble ingredients in an emulsion: butters, oils, and the emulsifying wax. Heated separately to the same temperature as the water phase before the two are combined.
The water-soluble ingredients in an emulsion: distilled water and water-soluble additives such as glycerin. Heated to the same temperature as the oil phase before combining.

content.text: . Both phases must reach the same temperature (70 to 75°C) before combining; pouring cold water into hot oil, or vice versa, breaks the emulsion. Peppermint essential oil goes in at the end, below 40°C, to preserve the volatile cooling compounds.

items.prepNote: Water phase; use distilled or freshly boiled and cooled
Oil phase; binds water and oil
Optional; 1% tea tree for additional foot care
Optional; add to water phase; softens callused skin

content.text: In the larger beaker (water phase): weigh distilled water and glycerin. In the smaller beaker (oil phase): weigh shea butter, coconut oil, jojoba oil, and emulsifying wax. Place both beakers in a simmering-water bath and heat to 72°C, monitoring both with the probe thermometer. Do not let either phase cool before combining.

With both phases at 70 to 75°C, pour the oil phase slowly into the water phase while using the stick blender at low speed. Blend for 2 minutes until the mixture turns white and thick. Continue stirring as it cools.

When the cream has cooled to below 40°C, add the peppermint essential oil and preservative. Stir well. Transfer to a clean pot or pump bottle. The cream will firm further as it cools to room temperature.
Apply a generous amount to clean, dry feet. Massage into heels and the balls of the feet. For a more intensive treatment, apply at night and wear cotton socks.
3 months at room temperature. Keep the lid closed between uses. Discard if the cream separates, smells rancid, or shows any sign of mould.
